The mutant is on line 89 and 90 of DateTimeZone.java. The original lines were:

if ( hoursOffset == 0 && minutesOffset == 0 ) {
    return DateTimeZone.UTC; }

And the mutant changed it to:

if ( false && minutesOffset == 0 ) {

So, essentially, the condition is now checking if 'false' and 'minutesOffset == 0', which will always be false. That means the code inside that if statement won't execute anymore.

Now, looking at the failing tests information. One test called TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear changed status from failing to passing because of this mutation. So, maybe fixing that particular issue caused it to pass. But for other tests related to DateTimeZoneCutover, their statuses didn't change—they remained failing.

Hmm, why would the month day property test be affected? Maybe the DateTimeZone's handling was causing some unexpected behavior in date calculations elsewhere. If the UTC check is bypassed now, perhaps that fixed an inconsistency when retrieving months or something like that.

As for whether this mutant is adjacent to a fault, I think so. Because changing the condition logic can have ripple effects on how time zones are handled, and some tests still fail because of it. So yes, this mutation likely introduced a bug.
